rationale_for_baseline_tier: A systemically well young adult with a classic benign self-limiting post-viral exanthem (herald patch then symmetrical scaly truncal eruption), no systemic toxicity, palms/soles spared and no new medication. The correct disposition is home self-care with symptomatic relief plus a ROUTINE GP appointment (1-2 weeks, or ~4 weeks per the source plan) to confirm the widespread rash clinically and to safety-net for the atypical mimics. It is not time-critical: there is no feature warranting same-day (T3), ED (T4) or ambulance (T5) assessment, and over-escalation would cause exactly the unnecessary panic and emergency presentation a good consultation should avert. Telehealth cannot confirm the confirmatory collarette scaling / distribution, which is why routine in-person confirmation is advised — but that limitation raises the need for confirmation, not the urgency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash spreads to the palms of the hands and/or the soles of the feet
escalation_edges.rationale: Palm/sole involvement raises secondary syphilis (and some drug eruptions) — a change in the diagnosis warranting prompt GP assessment and serology, though not an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash starts appearing on the palms of your hands or the soles of your feet, book to see a GP within a day or two so they can check for other causes and arrange a blood test if needed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Painful sores or ulcers develop in the mouth or on the genitals
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent same-day GP or urgent care review
escalation_edges.rationale: Mucosal ulceration points to erythema multiforme / Stevens-Johnson syndrome or secondary syphilis — none benign; warrants same-day assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get painful sores in your mouth or on your genitals, see a doctor the same day — go to an urgent care clinic or your GP that day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with joint pains or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features suggest a systemic infective or hypersensitivity process rather than a benign exanthem and need same-day assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever with aching joints, or you start feeling really unwell in yourself, get seen the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash rapidly worsens, blisters or the skin starts to peel (especially after any new medicine)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: Blistering / skin peeling raises a severe cutaneous adverse reaction (Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis) — a dermatological emergency requiring ED assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash suddenly gets much worse, forms blisters, or your skin starts peeling — especially if you've started any new medicine — go to an emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Itch becomes unbearable, prevents sleep and does not respond to antihistamines
escalation_edges.rationale: Refractory pruritus is not dangerous but warrants GP review for stronger symptomatic treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the itch becomes unbearable, stops you sleeping and the antihistamine isn't helping, see your GP in the next day or two for stronger treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is highly anxious about an unexplained widespread rash
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give explicit, warm reassurance and a clear written-style summary of what the rash is and what to watch for; suggest telling a friend or family member so they are not managing the worry alone, and offer a clear route back (routine GP, or 13 HEALTH for interim advice) so the isolation does not drive an unnecessary ED presentation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a benign, self-limiting skin condition that will settle on its own over several weeks. You can manage it at home: use a gentle soap-free wash and a bland moisturiser twice a day, take an antihistamine for the itch, keep showers short and lukewarm, and wear loose cotton clothing. Book a routine GP appointment in the next week or two (and again around 4 weeks) to confirm the diagnosis and check it is settling. You do not need an emergency department for this.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine appointment with your regular GP. For interim advice if you are unsure, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84), available 24/7.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is not an emergency. If a red-flag change occurs after hours (see the return-if list), call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage, or attend an urgent care clinic / emergency department for the blistering or mucosal-ulcer warning signs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP the timeline — the single larger patch first, then the widespread eruption a week later — the mild sore throat two weeks earlier, and that no new medicines were started. Mention the antibacterial soap you have been using.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the rash as trivial with no confirmation pathway and no safety-net at all — failing to advise a palm/sole check or GP confirmation, so a small chance of secondary syphilis or an evolving severe reaction goes unguarded.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient is told it is definitely nothing, never has the diagnosis confirmed, is not warned about danger signs, and a mimic (secondary syphilis or an evolving blistering reaction) is missed or acted on late.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Routing a benign self-limiting pityriasis rosea to urgent care, an emergency department or an ambulance because the rash looks dramatic and the patient is panicking — an unnecessary emergency presentation, wasted acute resources, and amplification of the patient's health anxiety, precisely the outcome the original consultation was designed to avert.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the diagnostic collarette scaling / cleavage-line distribution or examine for lymphadenopathy, and cannot itself exclude secondary syphilis with certainty.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Give a confident presumptive benign diagnosis with warm reassurance, while recommending routine in-person GP confirmation and a clear, specific safety-net for the atypical mimics (palm/sole spread, mucosal ulcers, systemic features, blistering). Do NOT over-escalate — the harm of a benign rash is low and the uncertainty is resolved by routine confirmation, not by emergency care.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either extreme: (a) alarming the patient by labelling it a possible STI or serious systemic illness and sending them to ED; or (b) falsely guaranteeing it is 'definitely nothing' with no confirmation and no safety-net, leaving the syphilis/severe-reaction mimics unguarded.
